statusLine: EU fuel stocks are under strain with 18 of 27 countries below the 90-day minimum in at least one fuel type, while oil prices have surged 32.9% since February amid Middle East disruptions.
fullAnalysis:
The EU's fuel security landscape shows significant stress across multiple member states. While EU averages remain above the mandatory 90-day minimum (petrol 113 days, diesel 102.2 days, jet fuel 96.4 days), the distribution is highly uneven. Romania faces the most severe challenges with all three fuel types in critical status, including diesel stocks at just 30.2 days. Poland and Luxembourg also show critical levels across all fuel categories.

Jet fuel emerges as the most vulnerable fuel type, with 16 of 27 countries below the 90-day threshold. This includes major economies like Germany (64.7 days) and France (67.5 days) in critical status. Diesel stocks are under pressure in 11 countries, notably affecting Spain (68.6 days) and Germany (64.3 days). The recent strategic reserve releases to address Middle East supply disruptions appear to have contributed to these depleted stock levels.

Brent crude oil's 32.9% surge since late February to $96.31 per barrel reflects ongoing supply concerns and adds cost pressure across the bloc. Price variations are substantial, with Dutch consumers paying €2.388 per liter for petrol compared to €1.34 in Malta. The combination of tight physical stocks and elevated prices suggests the EU fuel market remains vulnerable to further supply shocks.
keyPoints: 18 of 27 EU countries have at least one fuel type below the mandatory 90-day minimum reserves
Jet fuel is most vulnerable with 16 countries below threshold, including Germany and France in critical status
Romania shows critical levels across all three fuel types, with diesel stocks at only 30.2 days
Oil prices have surged 32.9% since February to $96.31/barrel amid Middle East supply disruptions
Significant price disparities exist across member states, with Dutch petrol prices 78% higher than Malta's
